question_text: A sound wave travels through air at 340 m/s. If the frequency of the sound is 85 Hz, calculate the wavelength of this sound wave. Then explain why this same sound would have a different wavelength if it travelled through water at 1500 m/s.
correct_answer: 4 m; The wavelength increases to approximately 17.6 m in water because the speed of sound is faster in water, and since frequency remains constant, a higher speed produces a longer wavelength (wavelength = speed ÷ frequency).
explanation: To find wavelength, divide the wave speed by the frequency: 340 ÷ 85 = 4 m. In water, the same sound has frequency 85 Hz but travels at 1500 m/s, so wavelength = 1500 ÷ 85 = 17.6 m. The frequency doesn't change when a wave enters a different medium, but the speed does, which changes the wavelength.
